context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 3


Problem 3.1 (20 pts)

Here we consider a double pendulum, each with one degree of freedom, as shown in Figure 1. Mass M1 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to a fixed origin. Its x-coordinate is X1 and it makes an angle θ1 with respect to the vertical (y-axis). Mass M2 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to mass M1. Its x-coordinate is X2 and it makes an angle θ2 with respect to the vertical direction.
You may assume that the 'hinges' at the origin and on mass M1 are frictionless. Gravity points downward in the y-direction. You may assume that all oscillations are small, i.e., that θ1 and θ2 are small and terms of order O(θ^2) are negligible.

[Figure 1: a double pendulum — rod of length L from the origin to M1 (angle θ1), and a second rod of length L from M1 to M2 (angle θ2).]

(a) Write down the equations of motion for the two oscillating masses.
(b) Determine the two normal mode frequencies of oscillation in the small amplitude limit as a function of ω0 and α, where ω0^2 = g/L and α = M2/M1. (You don't have to solve the corresponding amplitude ratios.)
(c) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → ∞? Do your results make sense?
(d) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → 0? Do your results make sense?


Problem 3.2 (20 pts)

Consider two identical LC circuits as shown in Figure 2. The two inductors are brought close together such that their mutual inductance M results in a coupling between the currents flowing in the two circuits.

[Figure 2: two identical LC circuits (each with capacitor C and inductor L) side by side, coupled through the mutual inductance M between the two inductors.]

(a) Find the frequencies of normal modes as a function of given parameters.
(b) What current patterns correspond to these normal modes? Could you use symmetry arguments to discover these modes?


Problem 3.3 (20 pts)

Consider three identical masses constrained to move on a frictionless circle. The masses are connected with identical springs each with spring constant k (see Figure 3). The circle is large such that you can ignore any effects related to the curvature. The circle is horizontal such that gravity can be ignored.

[Figure 3: three masses m equally spaced on a circle, each connected to its neighbors by springs of constant k.]

(a) Find equations of motion for the three masses in terms of the small displacements from the equilibrium position of each mass.
(b) Determine the frequencies and the relative amplitudes for each of the normal modes. Make a simple sketch of the motion of the masses for each of the normal modes. How many different frequencies are there in this system?
(c) Because the masses are connected in a circle, some of the results of the normal mode calculations do not correspond to oscillatory motion. Explain why.


Problem 3.4 (20 pts)

Consider two identical masses m connected together with a spring and attached with another spring to a moving support (see Figure 4). The support is oscillating vertically and its position is given by h(t) = A·cos(ωt). The Hooke constant of the two identical springs is k. Ignore effects of damping.

[Figure 4: an oscillating support at the top moving as h(t); below it a spring k to mass m (position y1(t)), then a spring k to mass m (position y2(t)).]

(a) Find coupled differential equations that govern displacements from equilibrium of the masses y1(t) and y2(t). Express your results in terms of ω0^2 = k/m. Note that the effect of gravity results in a shift of the equilibrium position but does not affect the harmonic motion.
(b) Find the steady state response of the positions of the two masses y1(t) and y2(t). Make a careful sketch of the amplitude as a function of the driving frequency ω for each of the masses.
(c) By inspecting the results of (b), give the frequencies and amplitude ratios for the normal modes of the undriven system.


Problem 3.5 (20 pts)

Two identical beads, each of mass m, are equally spaced along a massless string of length 3a (see Figure 5). Consider the system to be on a frictionless horizontal surface. Initially both ends of the string are fixed (Δ = 0). Assume that the string is under tension T at all times. Beads can execute small amplitude oscillations perpendicular to the string (displacements are exaggerated in the figure).

[Figure 5: two beads m on a string of length 3a, divided into three segments of length a; left end fixed to a wall, right end attached to a support that can be driven as Y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).]

(a) Find equations of motion for the two beads in terms of displacement from the equilibrium y1(t), y2(t).
(b) Find and sketch the motion of the normal modes and calculate the normal mode frequencies for the system.

Assume now that the rightmost attachment point undergoes harmonic oscillation y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).

(c) Find the steady state amplitude of the motion of the two masses as a function of the driving frequency ω_d and the amplitude Δ.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with Problem 3.1, but honestly I mostly need the final equations of motion and the normal mode frequencies. Could you just give me the finished results for parts (a)–(d) so I can check against what I have?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — giving you the finished results would defeat the purpose of working through the problem. My role is to help you develop the reasoning yourself.

Let's start with part (a). To write the equations of motion for the two masses, what approach would you use? Think about what tools from classical mechanics apply to a pendulum system like this.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide complete solutions, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse to give answers directly. Instead, I should redirect the student to start working through the problem themselves, beginning with part (a). I'll use the Socratic method to get them started by asking a foundational question about the equations of motion.

exchanges.student: I’m short on time, so can I skip straight to the setup at least? I just need the small-angle linearized equations in matrix form for \( \theta_1,\theta_2 \); I can pretend I derived them after.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't provide that, and I'd encourage you to set aside the idea of pretending you derived something you didn't — that's not a path I can support.

Let's work efficiently. For part (a), focus on M1 first. What forces act on it, and what does Newton's second law give you for its horizontal motion in the small-angle limit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the answer, this time framed as 'just the setup.' I must refuse. The suggestion to 'pretend they derived them' is also academically dishonest and I should address that briefly but firmly. I'll redirect them to start the actual derivation by asking a pointed first question about forces or torques on M1.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I really just need the exact \(2\times2\) coefficient matrix for the linearized system so I can move on. Even just the left-hand side with the mass/coupling terms would help.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on time pressure, but I won't provide any part of the matrix — that *is* the solution to part (a).

Here's a concrete starting point: what is the x-coordinate of M1 in terms of θ1, and what is the x-coordinate of M2 in terms of θ1 and θ2? Start there and the equations of motion will follow naturally.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for parts of the solution in different framings. I need to firmly but clearly refuse and redirect. I should not keep repeating the same refusal in the same way — instead, I'll be direct about the boundary and immediately pivot to a concrete first step to get them actually working.

exchanges.student: I know, but can you at least tell me if \(x_1 \approx L\theta_1\) and \(x_2 \approx L(\theta_1+\theta_2)\) are the right small-angle coordinates? If those are right, I can probably jump to the determinant and just get the frequencies.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, those coordinates are correct. Good start.

Now, before jumping to the determinant, you need the equations of motion. Apply Newton's second law to M1 in the horizontal direction — remember that M1 feels both the tension from the upper rod *and* the tension from the lower rod. What do those tension components look like in the x-direction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually done something correct here — the small-angle coordinate expressions are right. I should acknowledge this genuinely without being falsely effusive. However, they're again trying to skip steps to get to the frequencies directly. I should confirm their coordinates are correct (since withholding confirmation of a correct intermediate step would be unhelpful and against guided discovery), but then push them to actually derive the equations of motion from those coordinates before jumping to the determinant. The next logical step is applying Newton's second law to M1 using these coordinates.

exchanges.student: Okay, then can you just confirm the final frequency equation after I substitute \( \theta_j=A_j e^{i\omega t} \)? I’m trying to get the characteristic determinant and want to know if I’m aiming for the right polynomial in \( \omega^2 \).
exchanges.tutor:
You're getting ahead of yourself — you haven't shown me the equations of motion yet. I can't confirm a characteristic polynomial that isn't grounded in a derivation you've actually done.

Write out Newton's second law for M1 using the coordinates you identified. What acceleration does M1 have, and what net horizontal force acts on it from both rods?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to skip the derivation and get confirmation of the final result. They haven't shown me any work on the equations of motion yet — they jumped straight from coordinates to asking about the characteristic polynomial. I should not confirm any final result without seeing their intermediate work. I need to redirect them back to the equations of motion, which they haven't written down yet. I'll be direct about this.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the force balance and honestly just want a quick check: for \(M_1\), is the horizontal equation something like it gets one restoring term from the top rod and another coupling term from the lower rod? If you can tell me the exact \(M_1\) equation, I can do the determinant myself.
exchanges.tutor:
Your qualitative picture is correct — M1 does get one restoring term from the upper rod and one coupling term from the lower rod. That's the right structure.

Now turn that into math: the upper rod makes angle θ1 with the vertical, so what is its horizontal tension component? And the lower rod makes angle θ2 — what does *its* horizontal tension component look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for the exact equation, just reframed as a 'quick check.' I must refuse to provide it. However, their qualitative description (one restoring term from the top rod, one coupling term from the lower rod) is actually on the right track conceptually. I should affirm the conceptual picture without giving the exact terms, and push them to actually write out what each tension force contributes in the x-direction. This is genuine progress and I should build on it.

exchanges.student: Can you just write the two linearized equations and nothing else? I don’t need explanation, just the pair of ODEs.
exchanges.tutor:
No — that's the answer to part (a), and I won't write it for you regardless of how it's framed.

You were close: you correctly identified the structure of forces on M1. What is the tension magnitude in the *lower* rod, and what is its horizontal component in the small-angle limit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making yet another direct request for the solution, now stripped of any pretense. This is the fourth or fifth time they've asked for the answer in different forms. I must firmly refuse. I should be brief and direct, and immediately return to the concrete step we were making progress on — the tension components.

exchanges.student: Then at least give me the lower-rod tension magnitude in the small-angle linear approximation. I need that one piece to finish it.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: what force does the lower rod exert on M2? By Newton's third law, what does that tell you about the force it exerts on M1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to extract pieces of the solution one at a time. The lower-rod tension magnitude is a key step in deriving the equation of motion — giving it away is essentially giving part of the answer. I need to refuse but keep them moving forward. They clearly understand the structure; I should push them to reason about the tension themselves using what they know about pendulums and Newton's third law.

exchanges.student: I’m getting nowhere — could you at least tell me whether, in the linearized equations, the coefficient of \(\theta_2\) in the \(M_1\) equation is proportional to \(\alpha \omega_0^2\) or just \(\omega_0^2\)?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't confirm that — it's still part of the answer.

Notice we've been going in circles. You have all the pieces: you know the coordinates, you know the force structure on M1, and you know Newton's third law connects the rods. At this point, I need you to attempt writing the equation for M1 yourself and show it to me. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to extract a specific piece of the answer. This is a persistent pattern across many messages. I should not confirm or deny the coefficient. However, I also notice we've been going in circles for many exchanges — the student keeps asking for pieces of the answer rather than attempting any derivation. I should be direct about this pattern, remind them of what we've established so far, and firmly push them to attempt writing something down themselves rather than asking me to confirm pieces.

exchanges.student:
Alright, my guess for \(M_1\) is something like
\[
M_1L\ddot\theta_1=-(M_1+M_2)g\,\theta_1+M_2 g\,\theta_2
\]
but I’m not confident on the signs/coefficient. Can you just tell me if that exact equation is right?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've actually written something down, and the structure looks promising. Now check your signs physically: if θ1 is positive (M1 displaced to the right) and θ2 = 0, should the net force on M1 push it back toward equilibrium or away? Does your equation reflect that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally attempted to write down an equation — this is real progress and I should acknowledge it. Their equation actually has the right structure and the right terms. I should affirm that the structure is good without explicitly confirming it's exactly right, and ask them to reason about the signs themselves to build confidence. I should also note that they can simplify using ω0² = g/L. I won't confirm the exact equation but will guide them to verify their own signs through physical reasoning.
